# A06G FreeRTOS & I2C (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Mar 25, 2026 11:59 PM EDT

A **group** submission on Gradescope and GitHub Classroom.

**GitHub Classroom assignment:** [https://classroom.github.com/a/fnZ8s769](https://classroom.github.com/a/fnZ8s769) 

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* Better understand how I2C communication operates in embedded systems.  
* Become familiar with Simplicity Studio, software components, and its SDK.  
* Learn why we need additional debugging tools when working with RTOS \- and leverage Segger SystemView to do so.  
* Understand how to separate device functionality into tasks, and how tasks can communicate to each other, in an RTOS.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 

# 0.0 (Re)Configure Simplicity Studio & SDK

Software updates are great\! But not always… with new software comes new bugs\! We’ve done a fair bit of testing and found that the latest Simplicity Studio release and SDK have some issues (namely with Segger SystemView, our debugging tool). Before you get started on any firmware, you’ll need to ensure you’re running the recommended versions (**“version locking”**).

* Simplicity Studio Package 6.1.0-224  
* Simplicity SDK 2025.6.3

Ensure you have the latest [**Simplicity Installer**](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/install-ssv6/install-simplicity-studio) installed on your system. At time of writing, it is **v1.1.1-61**. You can review your currently installed versions under **Package Manager**.

| ![][image1] | ![][image2] |
| :---- | :---- |

Don’t have the correct versions? You can attempt to update manually (as below). There is also a way to import a “recipe” of packages to install. Your mileage may vary, but this has worked for some students: [ese5160\_v1\_siStudioInstallerRecipe.toml](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1UY-XUVJOs57dvJtpMKyOogZEcxzYGP3S/view?usp=drive_link)	  
![][image3]

## Manual Handling of the SDK & Simplicity Studio

**Simplicity SDK**

1. Click **Uninstall** for the version you currently have installed. This will make things easier when making in the future \- you won’t accidentally make a project with a different version.  
2. Click **Add new SDK**.  
3. Find **Simplicity SDK 2025.6.3** and click install.

**Simplicity Studio Package**

1. Click **Uninstall** for the version you currently have installed.  
2. Go back to the Simplicity Installer homepage and click on **Installation Wizard**, then **Advanced**.  
3. Scroll down to the **Tools** section, then click **Simplicity Studio Package 6.1.0-224**.  
4. After installation, resist the urge to update this\!

# 0.1 Update SiWx91x Connectivity Firmware

## Why do we need to update?

Recall that this wireless IC has two separate processors:

* Network Wireless Processor (**NWP**): handles all of the Wi-Fi, BLE, and other wireless communications  
* Cortex M4 (**M4**): runs the user application code

![][image4]

The NWP has a separate firmware image from the M4 \- and the M4’s user application code will send and receive messages from the NWP when it needs wireless handling. These messages are grouped together in an Application Programming Interface (API).

The SiLabs 917 wireless IC likely doesn’t have the latest NWP firmware \- meaning the API is out of date\! This is because manufacturing of these chips has happened months or years prior. Before we can use the Simplicity Studio’s latest wireless SDK and firmware project generation, we’ll need to load the latest firmware for the NWP.

| What happens if I don’t load the latest firmware on the NWP? You’ll see an error similar to this: *Unexpected error while initializing Wi-Fi: 0x16056* The M4 is attempting to initialize Wi-Fi through the NWP, but its API/SDK calls are a different version that what’s on the NWP. |
| :---- |

## Updating the Connectivity Firmware

1. Find where the connectivity firmware is stored on your computer, then remember the path and/or keep the window open. 

   The [Simplicity SDK 2025.6.3](https://docs.silabs.com/sisdk-release-notes/2025.6.3/sisdk-release-notes-overview/) leverages [WiSeConnect 3 SDK v3.5.2](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/3.5.2/sisdk-wifi-release-notes/) (or [v3.5.3](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/3.5.3/sisdk-wifi-release-notes/) in some cases?). Simplicity Studio is using [conan](https://conan.io/) as their package manager, which makes the file paths a little funny. If you’ve just uninstalled the old SDK and reinstalled the new SDK (2025.6.3), you can sort by **Date Modified** to find the correct instance of WiSeConnect.

   You can find the  path through Simplicity Studio under **Settings \> SDKs**.  
   ![][image5]  
   It’ll look something like this:  
   1. **Windows:** C:\\Users\\**user**\\.silabs\\slt\\installs\\conan\\p\\**wisecNNNNN**\\p\\connectivity\_firmware  
      ![][image6]  
   2. **Mac OS:** /Users/nmcgill/.silabs/slt/installs/conan/p/**wisecNNNNN**/p/  
   3. **Linux:** ??? (I don’t have a machine to test with\!)  
2. Connect your SiLabs 917 dev board / custom PCBA to your computer.  
3. Launch **Simplicity Device Manager** from Simplicity Studio (under **Tools**).  
   ![][image7]  
4. Find your device, then click **Configure**.  
   ![][image8]  
5. Now, browse to the file path you found earlier. Use the **standard** image. Click & drag it into **Install Custom Firmware**. Click the **Flash** button.  
   ![][image9]  
6. After a successful flash, you can click the **Read** button to ensure it’s the correct version. Now you are harmonized between your M4 and NPW\!  
   ![][image10]

Additional Resources  
[Using The Simplicity Studio Ide | Developing with Boards in SoC Mode | WiSeConnect | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/latest/wiseconnect-developers-guide-developing-for-silabs-hosts/using-the-simplicity-studio-ide#update-siwx91x-connectivity-firmware)   
[Index | troubleshooting Guide | WiSeConnect 3 | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/latest/wiseconnect-developers-guide-troubleshoot/)   
*Inside the* connectivity\_firmware *folder you can find the [README.md](http://README.md) file with detailed information about the all the connectivity firmware use case*

# 0.2 Update the Adapter Firmware 

You should  also update your adaptor firmware, either via Simplicity Device Manager or Simplicity Commander

| ![][image11] | ![][image12] |
| :---- | :---- |

# 

# 1\. I2C Communication

The objective of this section is to conceptually understand I2C  communication between two devices.

| (1.1) Using a diagram similar to the one to the right, show the order of messages for a typical write request sending the message “abc” to address “0xA8” | ![][image13] |
| :---- | :---: |

(1.2) Consider the following pseudocode:

| MCU 1 if( button\_pressed) {    // send I2C read data packet } else if(data\_received){    // if correct data is received blink on board led } | MCU 2 if( data\_received){    if (correct\_data\_is\_received) {        // turn on LED        // send ACK and “everyday”        // turn off LED    }    else {        // send NACK    } } |
| :---- | :---- |

Explain in chronological order the events when MCU 1 (from our pseudocode) has a button pushed. You can abbreviate the individual data messages, except for the first and last data messages.

(1.3) The following data packet is observed when a controller wants to write some data to a peripheral.  
Highlight the boxes (bytes) or box (byte) that are sampled when the controller controls the SDA line  
Identify which bytes are the:

* Peripheral address  
* Register address  
* Data byte

| S start | A6 | A5 | A4 | A3 | A2 | A1 | A0 | 0 r/w | A ack | B7 | B6 | B5 | B4 | B3 | B2 | B1 | B0 | A ack |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |

| D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 | A ack | P stop |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |

(1.4) Whenever an I2C controller or peripheral wants to send a low logic on the I2C line, it will pull it to ground, causing a short between the pull-up resistor and the I2C sensor’s ground. Why does this not harm the device?

Additional resource:  
[I2C Communication explained](https://youtu.be/6IAkYpmA1DQ?si=QGItyYoGDfDACEbt&t=164)

# 

# 2\.  Concurrency Challenge

## 2.1 Bare Metal Implementation

Recall the Concurrency Challenge introduced in [L10-S26 Embedded I - Concurrency.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1iusxFFioZHdC0ODiY_6gLktPM4IiYRZ0/view?usp=drive_link). The GitHub Classroom skeleton repository has a project named **concurrency\_challenge\_silabs** that implements a bare metal implementation for the SiLabs 917\. However, it’s missing the firmware implementation of:

* void button\_init\_task(void)  
* void button\_process\_task(void)

Complete those two functions to achieve the expected functionality of the Concurrency Challenge product requirements.

| Note: You can create multiple workspaces within Simplicity Studio, then switch between them. It might make your life easier to: Download the GitHub Classroom starter repository. Create a new workspace within that folder. Here’s a video demonstrating my recommended approach for setting up this assignment: [04 SiStudio Workspace with GitHub Repo Skeleton Projects.mp4](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1CRUmT0N0bvseas9g8v2A4Ev52tRD_LUv&usp=drive_fs) | ![][image14] ![][image15] |
| :---- | :---- |

## 

## Instruction added to GitHub and Fix for the  build error pushed to repo.

You can also just delete the toolchain.cmake file and it should work\!

| Adding \`cmake\` config to project Simplicity Studio Hardcodes the paths during the project generation so if you share the \`cmake\` config files with your teammate you might encounter issues with incorrect paths during build and flash. After adding the project in the Simplicity Studio. 1\. Open \`Manage Project\` 2\. Select \`Force Generate\` | ![][image16] |
| :---- | :---- |

(2.1) Commit your completed firmware to your GitHub Classroom repository.

| Use the project named concurrency\_challenge\_rtos for this remainder of this assignment. |
| :---- |

## 2.2 Segger SystemView

An RTOS can make system development much simpler and reliable \- but it certainly makes debugging the system more challenging. How is the kernel dispatching these tasks? How much processor time does each task get? A traditional debugger cannot answer these questions for us.

[Segger SystemView](https://www.segger.com/products/development-tools/systemview/) is a way of measuring these details. It’s a cross platform tool that SiLabs has licensed so its microcontrollers can use it for free.  
![][image17]  
It leverages a communication channel called [Segger Real Time Transfer (RTT)](https://www.segger.com/products/debug-probes/j-link/technology/about-real-time-transfer/) to quickly send data from the MCU, through the J-Link flasher/debugger, and to SystemView. It does this through the standard SWD interface \- the J-Link accesses a specific part of memory it set up for reading/writing debug information.

| ![][image18] | ![][image19] |
| :---- | :---- |

To use SystemView, we’ll need to:

1. Install Segger SystemView on your computer. [https://www.segger.com/downloads/systemview/](https://www.segger.com/downloads/systemview/)   
2. Add it as a Software Component in Simplicity Studio.  
3. Add a header file reference in the FreeRTOSConfig.h file.  
   1. (You’ll see a build error for this.)  
4. Add an include for a CMSIS function call.  
   1. (You’ll see a build error for this.)  
5. Flash your MCU, configure it in SystemView, then press play\!

I’ve detailed the process in this video. It creates a project from scratch \- you’ll leverage the existing project named   
[02 FreeRTOS with Segger SystemView.mov](https://drive.google.com/file/d/14igJIA1QEbuKV1zNI7X8D5cPUMoR3Frx/view?usp=drive_link)

## 2.3 RTOS Implementation

Now that you have a SystemView setup, you can leverage it as you port your bare metal concurrency code into a FreeRTOS ecosystem\! The **concurrency\_challenge\_rtos** skeleton project is based on the [WiSeConnect SL Si91x \- RTOS Mutex](https://github.com/SiliconLabs/wiseconnect/tree/master/examples/si91x_soc/cmsis-rtos/sl_si91x_mutex%20) example project that creates two LED control threads using [CMSIS-RTOS2](https://arm-software.github.io/CMSIS_5/RTOS2/html/index.html) overlaid on [FreeRTOS](https://www.freertos.org/). It’s your job to modify this example to implement the LED and Button tasks.

The following resources may come in handy\!

* [FreeRTOS queues](https://freertos.org/Documentation/02-Kernel/02-Kernel-features/02-Queues-mutexes-and-semaphores/01-Queues)   
* [Introduction to RTOS \- Solution to Part 5 (FreeRTOS Queue Example)](https://www.digikey.com/en/maker/projects/introduction-to-rtos-solution-to-part-5-freertos-queue-example/72d2b361f7b94e0691d947c7c29a03c9)   
* [sl\_main baremetal to RTOS Application Transition | Platform | latest | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/gecko-platform/latest/platform-service-sl-main-baremetal-to-kernel-application-transition/) 

(2.2) Commit your complete FreeRTOS Concurrency Challenge code to the GitHub Classroom repository.

(2.3) Commit a recording file & screenshot of Segger SystemView running with the RTOS Concurrency Challenge. The task information should be visible in the screenshot.

## 2.4 HighWaterMark / Stack Space

Now that you’ve completed the RTOS Concurrency Challenge, let’s see if the default stack space is appropriate for our application. Recall that, in an RTOS, the kernel uses the heap to assign stack space to each task. Because RAM is limited on embedded systems, it would be good to shrink the task stack as much as possible. However, overflowing the stack can be disastrous\!

To rectify both issues, it’s common to run a [high watermark (FreeRTOS specific)](https://www.freertos.org/Documentation/02-Kernel/04-API-references/03-Task-utilities/04-uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark) or [stack space (CMSIS-RTOS2 generalized)](https://arm-software.github.io/CMSIS_5/RTOS2/html/group__CMSIS__RTOS__ThreadMgmt.html#ga9c83bd5dd8de329701775d6ef7012720) test function. The goal is to run your embedded firmware application for a certain amount of time and through all of the expected functionality \- then check how much of your stack has been used. This number can then be leveraged to shrink your stack to an appropriate size for the task.

(2.4) Commit a screenshot of VS Code in debugging mode. It should be paused at a breakpoint and showing the high watermark / stack space value.  
It should look something like this:  
![][image20]

(2.5) In your README, report your stack usage details for the LED and button tasks.  
There will be two separate numbers, one for each task. Carefully review the high watermark / stack space functions you’re using. What value is it returning?

| At this point, you must submit your assignment to Gradescope as a PDF before continuing on to the TA checkoff. |
| :---- |

(2.6) After submitting to Gradescope, get checked off by a TA. You’ll be expected to show:

* Bare metal concurrency challenge  
* RTOS concurrency challenge  
* SystemView operation  
* HighWatermark/Stack space usage

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).

# Common Issues

**Question:**   
When creating the SystemView project (SI Labs mutex project successfully compiles/flashes), selecting the J-Link configuration either does nothing or produces the UART dialogue (asked for baud rate).  
From there, pressing play raises errors.  
![][image21]  
![][image22]

**Answer:**  
You may need to download [J-Link Software and Documentation Pack](https://www.segger.com/downloads/jlink/)
